main_ingredients: arrabbiata sauce (tomato, chilli, garlic, olive oil)

serving.description: 3 pieces of arancini with arrabbiata sauce
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 3 arancini pieces with arrabbiata dipping sauce, weighing approximately 300 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Rice balls, fried, with cheese filling (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite estimate)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for arancini exists. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central composite data for fried rice-based snacks with cheese filling (Arborio rice, mozzarella, Parmesan, breadcrumb coating, deep-fried). The input record's per-100g values (energy 250 kcal, protein 9.33 g, fat 15 g, carbohydrate 20 g) are consistent with this composite and are retained. Micronutrient values (calcium 100 mg, iron 0.83 mg, cholesterol 33.33 mg, saturated fat 6 g, trans fat 0.17 g, vitamin C 3.33 mg) are also consistent with USDA composite data for this food type and are retained. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and several vitamins/minerals are not reliably reported for this composite and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for arancini in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 69 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: white rice (Arborio-type, cooked), which has a published GI of approximately 69 per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The breadcrumb coating and deep-frying are expected to modestly raise the effective GI relative to plain boiled rice; however, the cheese filling and fat content slow gastric emptying, partially offsetting this. GI of 69 is used as a conservative medium-range estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (69 × 60 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 41.4.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg/100 g with minimal potassium and fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: At 400 mg sodium per 100 g (1,200 mg per 3-piece serving), arancini delivers 52–60% of the recommended daily sodium limit for hypertensive individuals in a single serving; patients should limit to 1 piece, request low-sodium sauce, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI Arborio rice and breadcrumb coating with low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated GI of 69 and glycemic load of 41.4 per 3-piece serving make arancini unsuitable for routine consumption by people with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed, portion should be limited to 1 piece, paired with non-starchy vegetables, and blood glucose monitored post-meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6 g/100 g, trans fat 0.17 g/100 g, sodium 400 mg/100 g, and deep-fried preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Deep-frying combined with mozzarella and Parmesan cheese contributes 18 g saturated fat and 0.51 g trans fat per 3-piece serving, both of which raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid or strictly limit arancini and choose baked alternatives where available.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); saturated fat 6 g/100 g exceeds RA threshold; tomato-based sauce contains nightshade compounds
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation generates pro-inflammatory AGEs and saturated fat from cheese exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid arancini or request a baked version with reduced cheese and nightshade-free sauce.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Arancini (singular: arancino or arancina) are traditional Sicilian deep-fried rice balls with origins in 10th-century Arab-Norman Sicily. Each ball is made from Arborio or short-grain risotto rice, shaped around a filling of mozzarella and Parmesan cheese, coated in seasoned breadcrumbs, and deep-fried until golden and crisp. This Singapore restaurant version is served as a set of 3 pieces accompanied by arrabbiata sauce — a spicy tomato-based condiment made with tomatoes, garlic, chilli, and olive oil. The exterior is crunchy and golden while the interior is creamy, cheesy, and slightly sticky from the starchy Arborio rice. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 250 kcal, 9.33 g protein, 15 g fat (6 g saturated), and 20 g carbohydrate with only 1.33 g dietary fibre. Sodium is high at 400 mg/100 g. The estimated glycemic index is 69 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 41.4 per 3-piece serving. Clinically, arancini are high concern for hypertension (high sodium), Type 2 Diabetes (high GI, high GL, low fibre), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat, trans fat, sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (AGEs from deep-frying, nightshade sauce). The dish is vegetarian but contains gluten, dairy, and egg allergens. It is not suitable for vegan, halal-certified, or gluten-free diets without modification.
common_user_queries: What is the glycemic index of arancini?
How much fat is in arancini?
Can I eat arancini if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact USDA FoodData Central entry for arancini exists. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA composite data for fried rice-based snacks with cheese filling and are consistent with the input record's values. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved-source food (Arborio-type white rice, GI 69, Atkinson et al. 2008). Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved source directly reports arancini as a named food item.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, mineral panel, B-vitamin panel, and moisture data for deep-fried arancini with cheese filling are not available as a named composite in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. All core macros (protein_g 9.33, fat_g 15.0, carbohydrate_g 20.0) are non-zero and sourced from USDA composite adaptation; no zero-macro justification is required. GI of 69 is an analogous estimate based on Arborio-type white rice per Atkinson et al. 2008 (University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care). The dish is sold on GrabFood Singapore and is classified as a restaurant-prepared Italian snack. Halal status is unconfirmed — no halal certification information is available from the source listing. The input record listed 'country_of_origin: Singapore' reflecting the restaurant location, not the dish's culinary origin (Sicily, Italy); this has been corrected in region_of_origin. Multilingual names for Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are not provided as arancini is an Italian dish served in Singapore without established local-language names in those scripts; Chinese names are provided as the dish appears on a Singapore food delivery platform.
